_meta.description: Omega MegaCorp Design Intelligence Knowledge Base

entries.name: SHINY.NEW — Omega MegaCorp
entries.what_makes_it_special: Pure state machine replaces scroll — 8 scenes fire on timer, forcing forward-only pacing. Conic-gradient rotated via @property CSS custom property because GSAP cannot tween gradient strings directly.
entries.the_unexpected: The artifact reveal is decided silently on §3 submit — all of §4-§6 is pure theater for a decision already made.
entries.brief_injection: Make the system's decision feel like fate — compute the outcome first, then stage the theater of it arriving.

entries.name: Lusion
entries.what_makes_it_special: WebGL/Three.js 3D environments respond to scroll with morphing scenes — 3D content is structural, not decorative. Scroll triggers geometry deformation, not just parallax.
entries.the_unexpected: 3D scenes morph as you scroll as if the geometry is alive — not pre-rendered video, real-time WebGL that responds to your exact scroll position.
entries.brief_injection: Make 3D content respond to user input like a physical object — deform it, don't just move it.

entries.name: Active Theory
entries.what_makes_it_special: Case studies presented like film trailers — scroll-triggered theatrical reveals build anticipation before showing any content. Navigation is choreography, not wayfinding.
entries.the_unexpected: The case study page loads with 4 seconds of pure atmosphere before showing a single piece of work — the anticipation is the product.
entries.brief_injection: Build anticipation before revealing content — 3-4 seconds of atmosphere before the product appears makes the reveal feel earned.

entries.name: Uncommon Studio
entries.what_makes_it_special: Animation timing creates a rhythm that guides viewer attention — staggered grid reveals with precisely calibrated delays make the page feel like it breathes rather than loads.
entries.the_unexpected: The page feels like it has a heartbeat — each element arrives at a tempo that feels musical, not mechanical.
entries.brief_injection: Calibrate animation stagger so the page has a tempo — elements should arrive like a melody, not a dump.

entries.name: Epic
entries.what_makes_it_special: Typography morphs and shifts as you scroll — letter-spacing, size, and weight are scroll-driven variables, not static properties. The type IS the animation.
entries.the_unexpected: Font weight and letter-spacing change continuously as you scroll — the same text reads differently at different scroll positions, creating meaning through form.
entries.brief_injection: Use variable fonts with scroll-driven axes so the typography itself is the primary animation — weight, width, and spacing as motion design.

entries.name: BDSN Club
entries.what_makes_it_special: Generative art responds to cursor position in real-time — the entire canvas recomputes based on where your mouse is, not just on hover states.
entries.the_unexpected: The entire background is a different generative system every time you visit — it seeds from the current timestamp, so no two sessions look the same.
entries.brief_injection: Let the cursor be a brush — the user's mouse position should continuously alter the visual environment, not just trigger hover states.

entries.name: Stuff by Kris Temmerman
entries.what_makes_it_special: Portfolio is also a game — WebGL + GSAP used to create interactive experiments that ARE the portfolio pieces, not images of them. Winner of SOTD March 2026.
entries.the_unexpected: You can play the portfolio — each case study is interactive. The navigation itself is a mini-game. This turns browsing into doing.
entries.brief_injection: Make the interface a toy — if users can play with it rather than read it, they will stay 10x longer and remember 10x more.

entries.name: Unseen Studio
entries.what_makes_it_special: Full-screen navigation reveal on hover — instead of a dropdown, the entire viewport transforms into the nav. Navigation as spatial transition, not UI element.
entries.the_unexpected: Hovering the menu trigger causes the entire page to transform — not a dropdown, a full viewport takeover that you navigate through.
entries.brief_injection: Navigation can be a spatial transition, not a UI dropdown — let the menu take over the whole viewport and become the environment.

entries.name: Utopia Tokyo
entries.what_makes_it_special: Surveillance-themed cyberpunk experience using CSS clip-path polygons for angular deconstructed hero frames, a three-layer glitch text system built entirely in CSS (no JS canvas) using staggered clip-path polygon animations with RGB drop-shadow color shifting, and an SVG radar visualization with animated stroke-dasharray. The entire glitch system is accessible and reduced-motion compliant.
entries.the_unexpected: The entire glitch system — scanlines, noise, RGB text split, character scramble — is implemented in pure CSS with zero canvas or WebGL, making it fully accessible and reduced-motion compliant.
entries.brief_injection: Build the hero section using clip-path polygons to fracture the frame into angular shards, layer CSS keyframe scanline and noise animations over a dark base, and trigger full-palette theme flips via data-attribute cascades through custom properties on hover.

entries.name: San Rita
entries.what_makes_it_special: The entire site is structured as an interactive topographical map — navigation is spatial, not hierarchical. Sections are labeled as trails, fishing spots, and viewpoints. The loader sequence stages progressive UI revelation as terrain terraforming: 'Atmosphere deployment → Geosystem creation.' Built on Next.js with Lenis smooth scroll.
entries.the_unexpected: The loader doesn't say 'Loading...' — it narrates a world being built: 'Terraforming activated → Atmosphere deployment → Geosystem creation,' reframing a technical wait as environmental immersion.
entries.brief_injection: Replace conventional nav with a spatial map interface where sections are named as terrain features (trails, viewpoints, camps), and stage the page load as a terraforming sequence with progressive narrative copy rather than a spinner.

entries.name: The Obsidian Assembly
entries.what_makes_it_special: Uses @property (Houdini CSS) for animatable custom properties, character-by-character text reveals via --char-index/--line-index CSS variables, and a scroll-linked --progress custom property driving CSS-native parallax with no JavaScript touching transforms.
entries.the_unexpected: The parallax is entirely CSS-driven through a --progress custom property bound to scroll position, with no JavaScript touching transforms — Houdini @property enables smooth interpolation of the numeric value.
entries.brief_injection: Drive all scroll animations through a single --progress CSS custom property registered via @property, then use it across clip-path, translate, and opacity to create CSS-native parallax without a single JS scroll listener.

entries.name: Arnaud Rocca Portfolio
entries.what_makes_it_special: Hero display type scaled via clamp() from 56px to 308px — a nearly 6x range in a single rule, making the headline the entire composition at wide viewports. Per-project brand color metadata drives full --color-theme-* custom property resets as you scroll between projects.
entries.the_unexpected: The display headline scales 6x in physical size using a single clamp() rule — at the widest viewport it reads at 308px, becoming the entire composition rather than just a heading element.
entries.brief_injection: Set your hero display type with clamp(56px, 20vw, 308px) so the headline becomes the layout at wide viewports, and let each content section carry its own --color-theme-* custom properties so the palette fully resets as you scroll between projects.

entries.name: And And And Studio
entries.what_makes_it_special: Uses the View Transitions API for native browser page transitions with no GSAP or Framer Motion, plus a 400vh floating parallax gallery. Default gallery state is full grayscale — hover reveals color, inverting the typical highlight pattern so interaction feels like developing a photograph.
entries.the_unexpected: The gallery defaults to full grayscale and hover reveals color — the opposite of every standard hover effect — making the interaction feel like developing a photograph rather than selecting an item.
entries.brief_injection: Apply grayscale(1) to all gallery items by default and transition to grayscale(0) on hover, so browsing feels like selectively developing photographs from a contact sheet rather than picking from a color grid.
